chunks.text: Claire Tomlin is a Professor at UC Berkeley EECS, holding the James and Katherine Lau Chair in Engineering. Her research focuses on hybrid systems, distributed and decentralized optimization, and control theory, with applications in unmanned aerial vehicles, air traffic control, and biological process modeling. She previously taught at Stanford University from 1998 to 2007, directing the Hybrid Systems Laboratory and holding joint positions in Aeronautics and Astronautics and Electrical Engineering.

Claire Tomlin earned her Ph.D. in EECS from UC Berkeley in 1998, an M.Sc. in Electrical Engineering from Imperial College, London in 1993, and a B.A.Sc. in Electrical Engineering from the University of Waterloo in 1992. Her research areas include Control, Intelligent Systems, and Robotics (CIR) and Biosystems & Computational Biology (BIO), with specific focus on control theory, hybrid and embedded systems, and biological cell networks.

Claire Tomlin is affiliated with several research centers at UC Berkeley, including the Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research Lab (BAIR), Berkeley Center for New Media (BCNM), Center for Neural Engineering & Prostheses (CNEP), Center for the Theoretical Foundations of Learning, Inference, Information, Intelligence, Mathematics and Microeconomics at Berkeley (CLIMB), Foundations Of Resilient CybEr-physical Systems (FORCES), and Verified Human Interfaces, Control, and Learning for Semi-Autonomous Systems (VeHICaL).

Claire Tomlin has received numerous honors and awards, including the IEEE Mildred Dresselhaus Medal (2024), IFAC Fellow (2020), American Academy of Arts and Sciences Member (2019), National Academy of Engineering Member (2019), IEEE Transportation Technologies Award (2017), Outstanding Advising Administrator (2017), AIMBE Fellow (2015), Electrical Engineering Teaching Award (2011), IEEE Fellow (2010), Okawa Research Grant (2006), MacArthur Fellow (2006), Donald P. Eckman Award (2003), and MIT Tech Review TR100 (2003).
